question: Does EAD field presence predict actual prediction quality (degree of corroboration)?
method: Run test_severity.py on 679 experiments. Compare severity scores for experiments with/without EAD fields. Classify verdict distribution by severity bucket.
prediction: EAD prediction quality correlates with field presence <0.3 — most lanes have fields but poor prediction accuracy
falsification: Severity lift from field presence >0.3 AND mean severity with fields >0.4

key_patterns: Severity inversion: high-severity tests produce 3.8x more falsifications than confirmations
Field presence is necessary but not sufficient: having expect field adds +0.244 severity, but mean is still 0.301
Falsification field is rarest (12/679 = 1.8%) but highest-impact (+0.118 lift)
52% of experiments have UNKNOWN verdict — format not standardized enough for automated classification

verdict: PARTIALLY FALSIFIED. Expect-field lift is +0.244 (predicted <0.3 — close but within range). However the prediction was directionally wrong: field presence IS a meaningful predictor of quality (not weak). The deeper finding: PCI's field-presence metric is directionally correct but insufficient — it captures necessary condition (has fields) but not sufficient (fields are good). Mean severity 0.225 even with fields means 86.2% of confirmations are tautological.
key_finding: The swarm's scientific process has a severity inversion: easy tests get confirmed (18% confirmation at low severity), hard tests get falsified (19% falsification at high severity). This means the confirmation rate (87/679 = 12.8%) overstates epistemic progress — most confirmations are weak corroboration of things that couldn't reasonably have failed. Falsifications, though rarer (52/679 = 7.7%), carry 3.8x more epistemic weight per test.
self_test: This experiment scored its own prediction: specificity moderate (quantitative <0.3 threshold), riskiness moderate (testing against existing PCI), outcome partially falsified. Self-severity estimate: 0.35-0.40.
